description: PRE-REGISTERED by the author, 2026-07-13, before any Stage 8b analysis ran. The lineup-fit question: do teams accept weaker bats where their park asks more of a fielding position?

registration.unit: team-season x fielding position (2-9), home-game lineups from plays.csv bat_f
registration.outcome: PA-weighted mean full-sample tau_bat of players fielded at the position, demeaned by season x position
registration.regressor: park positional demand = Stage 8 compositional putout-share effect (park x position), standardized within position
registration.control: park overall career effect (guards the Coors confound: hitters' parks correlating with both demand and roster construction)
registration.prediction: negative overall slope; signal carried by the OUTFIELD CORNERS (slugger parked in front of a short wall - Fenway/Minute Maid LF, Baker Bowl RF), CF secondary; nulls at 1B and middle infield
registration.magnitude: expected 3-5 points of wOBA-skill per SD of park demand at the most differentiated positions
registration.detection: overall slope negative at >= 2 SE (cluster-robust by park). Below 2 SE is a publishable null absorbed by the section's 'the effect should be small' framing. Positive at >= 2 SE means the confound control failed; reported as unresolved, not spun.
registration.auxiliary: home vs road decomposition, informational only: roster-level fit shows in road lineups too; usage-level fit makes home exceed road
registration.scope: reduced form (skill-on-demand regression, clustered SEs), not the censored-share Tobit; the appendix gains one clause noting this

notes: The corners emphasis (amended from the manuscript TK's center-field emphasis) follows from the Stage 6 hand-x-type discovery: a short wall lowers the defensive cost and raises the offensive payoff for the same player type simultaneously.
